sentences.base_language: I wake up at seven o'clock.
sentences.grammarNote: Time expressions use the particle に to mark a specific point in time: 七時に (at seven o'clock). However, relative time words like 今日 (today), 明日 (tomorrow), and 毎日 (every day) do NOT use に — they stand alone.

sentences.target_language: それから顔を洗います。
sentences.grammarNote: Japanese uses the body part as a direct object with を: 顔を洗う (wash face), 歯を磨く (brush teeth). Unlike English, no possessive 'my' is needed — context makes it clear whose body part is meant.

sentences.target_language: 家で朝ごはんを食べます。
sentences.grammarNote: The particle で marks the location where an action takes place: 家で食べる (eat at home), 学校で勉強する (study at school). Use に for existence and destination; use で for the scene of action.

sentences.base_language: I drink a cup of coffee.
sentences.target_language: コーヒーを一杯飲みます。
sentences.grammarNote: 杯 (hai/pai/bai) is the counter for cups and glasses of liquid: 一杯 (ippai), 二杯 (nihai), 三杯 (sanbai). Japanese counters change pronunciation depending on the number — this sound change (rendaku) is common.

sentences.base_language: I leave the house at eight.
I take the subway to work.
sentences.target_language: 地下鉄で会社に行きます。
sentences.grammarNote: The particle で (de) marks the means of transport: 地下鉄で (by subway), バスで (by bus), 歩いて (on foot — note: 歩いて uses て-form, not で). Destination uses に: 会社に行く (go to the office).

sentences.base_language: I arrive at the office at nine.
sentences.target_language: 九時にオフィスに着きます。
sentences.literal: Nine o'clock at office at arrive.
